content.text: Based on the official Claude documentation and resources,
in the Agent SDK in Production refers to
the mechanisms for managing conversation history and agent context across multiple interactions, enabling agents to maintain state and resume work precisely where they left off
— critical for production deployments where agents handle long-running tasks.
A session is an agent instance within an environment
that maintains conversation history across multiple interactions.

Session = Agent + Environment + Conversation History
    ↓
Tracks progress while events drive execution
    ↓
Continues conversation across multiple user interactions
: "Sessions allow you to continue conversations across multiple interactions while maintaining full context".
SDK automatically creates session, returns session ID in initial system message
Create session to provision its sandbox
Send user event to start work

# Python: Use resume option with session ID
result = agent.query(prompt, resume=session_id)
: "The SDK automatically handles loading the conversation history and context when you resume a session, allowing Claude to continue exactly where it left off".
Create a new session from a resumed state
: "Create a new session ID that starts from the resumed state".

# Default: InMemorySessionStore
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent

agent = Agent()  # Uses InMemorySessionStore by default
. The SDK marks this explicitly as
, because all session state is lost when the process exits".
: "The SDK ships examples for file-based, Redis, PostgreSQL, and S3 stores".
Session state is the agent's scratchpad during that interaction

Each session's state contains:
- Key-value pairs
- Values updated by agent throughout session
- Files read, analysis performed, decisions reached
: "The agent has full context from everything that happened earlier in the same session: files it has read, analysis it has performed, decisions it has reached".
State properties are effectively key-value pairs
Properties organized into buckets (User state, Conversation state)
Agent reads/writes through state management object
: "Session state is keyed by session ID, not by the person who triggered it. There is no user-identity layer in the SDK's session model".

# Python: Manage state properties
from claude_agent_sdk import Agent

agent = Agent()

# Read state
state_value = agent.state.get("user_preference")

# Write state
agent.state.set("user_preference", "dark_mode")
: "The agent SDK manages session state for you, so keeping track of the session identifier is all that's necessary to continue an existing session".
For production-grade agents, store session data outside the agent's runtime

# Python: Use DatabaseSessionService
from google.adk import SessionService

session_service = DatabaseSessionService(
    database_uri="postgresql://user:password@localhost/db"
)
: "The Claude Agents SDK tracks session state but not user context across sessions".
: "A mechanism for building user-specific context that persists and grows across sessions".
: Use external memory systems like Mem0 to add persistent, per-user memory.

: each session is a single phone call with the agent. The session ID is the call number. When you hang up (end session), you can call back later using the same call number (resume), and the agent will remember everything from that previous call. Session state is like a scratchpad the agent uses during the call — notes, decisions, files opened. But once the call ends, the scratchpad is cleared (unless you use persistent storage). Long-term memory is like writing notes in a permanent notebook that persists across all calls.
Session = agent instance maintaining conversation history across multiple interactions
Session ID returned on ResultMessage: capture it to resume later
option (Python/TypeScript) with session ID to continue where left off
Fork: create new session ID from resumed state (
InMemorySessionStore: built-in, NOT for production (state lost when process exits)
Production storage: implement SessionStore protocol with file-based, Redis, PostgreSQL, or S3
Session state: agent's scratchpad (key-value pairs, files read, analysis, decisions)
Session state keyed by session ID, not user identity: no user-identity layer in SDK
State management: key-value pairs organized into scoped buckets (User state, Conversation state)
Session state = within session only; Long-term memory = across sessions (requires external implementation)
Agent SDK manages session state: keeping session identifier is all that's necessary to continue
"The agent SDK manages session state for you, so keeping track of the session identifier is all that's necessary to continue an existing session" — the absolute minimum for session continuity.
When working with sessions & state, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with sessions & state. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of sessions & state scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
